title: Meta-analysis: Mining 975 Expensive Training Runs (Part 1/3)
author: abaybektursun
body:
**Site:** https://abaybektursun.github.io/parameter-golf/meta_analysis/site/meta.html

---

## The idea

8×H100 time is expensive. But 975 teams already burned that compute, and all the logs are public. Can we mine this data to predict how a submission will perform on 8×H100s *without* actually running 8×H100s?

Three ways this could work:

1. **From early checkpoints** — if the outcome is visible at step 1,000, you can kill bad runs early and save 85% of the compute
2. **From source code** — if technique choices and architecture features predict the final score, you can estimate BPB before training starts
3. **From cheaper hardware** — if a quick run on a single A6000 or RTX 5090 correlates with 8×H100 results, you never need the expensive machine for exploration

## The three parts

### Part 1: Mining 975 Expensive Training Runs (this PR)

Built a database from all 409 submissions to the 8×H100 track. Classified every PR using a three-step pipeline:

1. A Claude agent examined PRs iteratively and built a taxonomy from scratch — 8 strategies, 49 techniques, 5 violation types
2. A script tags each PR against that taxonomy (one LLM call per PR, structured output)
3. A deterministic script merges tags + training logs + architecture metadata into the final database

Results: 264 valid, 132 invalid (93% of N-gram submissions broke the rules), 13 unauditable.

Key findings:
- The shared template ("Domination") turned 975 runs into a natural controlled experiment
- BigramHash, EMA, XSA had the strongest technique associations. Stacking more techniques stops helping after ~10
- 25–30M parameters at int6 is the sweet spot under the 16MB artifact limit
- **Early BPB at step 1,000 correlates 0.86 with final BPB.** Seed variance is 0.5 mBPB. Architecture determines the outcome and you can see it 90 seconds into a 10-minute run

That last finding is what makes Parts 2 and 3 possible.

### Part 2: The 1,000-Step Oracle (coming soon)

A model that predicts final BPB from early training checkpoints. Can you know the outcome before the run finishes?

### Part 3: Cross-Hardware Prediction (coming soon)

Train on an A6000 or RTX 5090, predict what 8×H100s would produce. If this works, you only burn expensive compute on the submissions that are actually going to win.
